coredata.dc:title: Temperature-modulated porous gadolinium micro-networks with hyperchrome-enhanced fluorescence effect
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Bin-Bin
Chang, Shuai
Lv, Jian
Qian, Ruo-Can
Li, Da-Wei
coredata.dc:description: Porous and luminescent functional materials having well-controlled morphology are crucial in the field of adsorption, catalysis, and drug release. Herein, we propose the mechanism of hyperchrome-enhanced fluorescence (HEF) for the synthesis of highly fluorescent gadolinium micro-networks (Gd-MNs) via a facile one-pot hydrothermal treatment of almost non-luminescent Gd3+ ions / citric acid (Gd3+/CA) complexes. It is found that after high-temperature polymerization, a strong π-π* transition occurs in the Gd-MNs, which significantly enhances the absorption of light. So, our proposed HEF effect as a new photoactivation strategy can efficiently improve the fluorescence (FL) emission of optical materials by creating a large π-conjugated structure. Surprisingly, the FL of Gd-MNs is 152 times higher compared to that of Gd3+/CA complexes. In particular, porous Gd-MNs (P-Gd-MNs) having tunable porosity can be obtained by changing the reaction temperature, as the formation of the porous structure is strongly dependent on the release of H2O molecules, coordinated with Gd3+ ions. Because of their porous structure and good FL properties, P-Gd-MNs is an ideal material for white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) with adjustable correlation color temperature, by embedding fluorescent carbon dots into their pores. Besides, P-Gd-MNs can also be transformed into a peroxidase-like catalyst by directly reducing the Cu2+ ions of the porous surface. Moreover, Cu coated P-Gd-MNs can also be used for catalysing the degradation of rhodamine 6G dye with a degradation efficiency of about 100%. Hence, the facile synthesis of P-Gd-MNs opens new avenues for its potential applications in WLEDs fabrication and biocatalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gadolinium micro-networks
Hyperchrome-enhanced fluorescence
Temperature-modulated pore structure
Peroxidase-like catalysis
White light-emitting diodes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Degradation process of R6G using Cu@P-Gd-MNs
Hyperchrome-enhanced fluorescence effect of Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs
CDs@P-Gd-MNs for WLEDs with adjustable CCTs
Cu@P-Gd-MNs as peroxidase-like catalyst for R6G dye degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Growth mechanism and morphology of Gd-MNs. (a) Illustration of the synthesis process of Gd-MNs and their corresponding SEM images of each reaction stage. Formation process of the Gd-MNs: (i) Gd3+/CA coordination, (ii) high temperature-induced disordered aggregation (2D microsheets appeared at about 1.5 h), (iii) appearance of ‘‘hot-nodes’’ (appeared at about 2 h), (iv) the growth of ‘‘hot-nodes’’ by the Ostwald ripening process (appeared at about 2.5 h), (v) appearance and growth of Gd-MNs (appeared at about 3.5 h), and (vi) final formation of Gd-MNs (about 5 h). (b) FT-IR spectra of CA, Gd3+/CA complex and Gd-MNs (from top to down: CA (brown line), Gd3+/CA complex (purple line) and Gd-MNs (blue line)); (c, d) SEM images of Gd-MNs; (e) SEM image of the “hot-nodes” of Gd-MNs; (f) the enlarged SEM image of “hot-nodes” marked with yellow solid frame in (e); (g) SAED pattern of Gd-MNs; (h) XRD spectrum of Gd-MNs; (i) element mapping of Gd-MNs; (j) XPS spectrum of Gd-MNs.

Structure and formation mechanism of P-Gd-MNs. (a) The temperature-dependent porous structure of P-Gd-MNs. Reaction time: 5 h; (b) hole size distribution; (c) SEM image of P-Gd-MNs prepared at 240 °C; (d) SAED pattern of P-Gd-MNs; (e) XRD spectrum of P-Gd-MNs; (f) FT-IR spectrum of P-Gd-MNs. (g) thermogravimetric curves of Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs; (h) element ratio of Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs; (i) illustration of the formation process of P-Gd-MNs.

Optical properties of Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs. (a) FL confocal images of Gd-MNs. Excitation wavelength: 405 nm. (b) Absolute QYs of Gd3+/CA complex, Gd-MNs, and P-Gd-MNs; (c) absorption spectra of CA, Gd3+/CA complex, Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs; (d) the emission spectra of CA, Gd3+/CA complex, Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs. The excitation wavelengths of CA, Gd3+/CA complex, Gd-MNs, and P-Gd-MNs are 330, 350, 370, and 370 nm, respectively. (e) FL lifetimes of Gd3+/CA complex, Gd-MNs, and P-Gd-MNs; (f) Schematic diagram of HEF effect.

Device performance of fabricated WLEDs. (a) Schematic diagram of WLEDs with different CCTs. (b) The chromaticity coordinates of Gd-WLEDs, P-WLEDs, CDs-1@P-WLEDs, CDs-2@P-WLEDs, and CDs-3@P-WLEDs with CCTs of 15427, 8431, 6866, 4514, and 2026 K under a drive current of 60 mA in Commission Internationale de l’E’clairage (CIE) 1931 colour spaces. (c) Photographs of the as-fabricated P-WLEDs (i), CDs-1@P-WLEDs (ii), CDs-2@P-WLEDs (iii) and CDs-3@P-WLEDs (iv). (d) SEM image and (e) element mapping of CDs-3@P-Gd-MNs.

The catalytic process of Cu@P-Gd-MNs. (a) Schematic diagram of the synthesis and catalytic process of Cu@P-Gd-MNs. (b) SEM image and element mapping of Cu@P-Gd-MNs. (c) Time-dependent degradation rate at different concentrations of H2O2 and various amounts of Cu@P-Gd-MNs. (d, e) ESR spectra of Cu@P-Gd-MNs in the presence of H2O2.

head.ce:title: Temperature-modulated porous gadolinium micro-networks with hyperchrome-enhanced fluorescence effect

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials, Feringa Nobel Prize Scientist Joint Research Center, Frontiers Science Center for Materiobiology & Dynamic Chemistry, School of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Feringa Nobel Prize Scientist Joint Research Center
Frontiers Science Center for Materiobiology & Dynamic Chemistry
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials, Feringa Nobel Prize Scientist Joint Research Center, Frontiers Science Center for Materiobiology & Dynamic Chemistry, School of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200237, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors: Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials, Feringa Nobel Prize Scientist Joint Research Center, Frontiers Science Center for Materiobiology & Dynamic Chemistry, School of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200237, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Feringa Nobel Prize Scientist Joint Research Center
Frontiers Science Center for Materiobiology & Dynamic Chemistry
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Porous gadolinium micro-networks with hyperchrome-enhanced fluorescence effect are prepared for white light-emitting diodes and highly-efficient peroxidase-like catalyst.

Porous and luminescent functional materials having well-controlled morphology are crucial in the field of adsorption, catalysis, and drug release. Herein, we propose the mechanism of hyperchrome-enhanced fluorescence (HEF) for the synthesis of highly fluorescent gadolinium micro-networks (Gd-MNs) via a facile one-pot hydrothermal treatment of almost non-luminescent Gd3+ ions / citric acid (Gd3+/CA) complexes. It is found that after high-temperature polymerization, a strong π-π* transition occurs in the Gd-MNs, which significantly enhances the absorption of light. So, our proposed HEF effect as a new photoactivation strategy can efficiently improve the fluorescence (FL) emission of optical materials by creating a large π-conjugated structure. Surprisingly, the FL of Gd-MNs is 152 times higher compared to that of Gd3+/CA complexes. In particular, porous Gd-MNs (P-Gd-MNs) having tunable porosity can be obtained by changing the reaction temperature, as the formation of the porous structure is strongly dependent on the release of H2O molecules, coordinated with Gd3+ ions. Because of their porous structure and good FL properties, P-Gd-MNs is an ideal material for white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) with adjustable correlation color temperature, by embedding fluorescent carbon dots into their pores. Besides, P-Gd-MNs can also be transformed into a peroxidase-like catalyst by directly reducing the Cu2+ ions of the porous surface. Moreover, Cu coated P-Gd-MNs can also be used for catalysing the degradation of rhodamine 6G dye with a degradation efficiency of about 100%. Hence, the facile synthesis of P-Gd-MNs opens new avenues for its potential applications in WLEDs fabrication and biocatalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Functional materials with superior fluorescence (FL) are of scientific and technological interest due to their unique optical properties, good chemical stability, and excellent electron transfer reaction [1–3]. These materials have been widely employed in the field of optoelectronic devices [4], chemo-/biosensing [5], catalysis [6], and drug delivery [7]. Generally, highly-luminescent micro/nanomaterials can be prepared by common photoactivation strategies such as aggregation-induced emission (AIE) [8–10], clusterization-triggered emission (CTE) through space conjugation [11–13], crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) [14], antenna effect [15]. In most of these methods, aromatic compounds with strong π-π* transition are used as starting materials. For instance, most of the AIE luminogens (AIEgens) contain aromatic structures, which can easily generate strong FL by the restriction of intramolecular motions [8]. On the other hand, the antenna effect uses organic ligands with aromatic structures and can effectively sensitize the luminescence of rare-earth ions [15]. Recently, organic molecules with aromatic moieties are preferred in the synthetic design of highly luminescent materials, while the “atypical” organic molecules without large π-conjugated structures are ignored.

These “atypical” organic molecules such as fatty acids have only n-π* transition, which is non-fluorescent in the solution state due to their low quantum efficiency. Nevertheless, these “atypical” organic molecules contain abundant chromophores, such as C = C, C = O, N = N, and N = O bonds. The great luminescent potential must be exploited and utilized, as the mass accumulation or aggregation of chromophores is an effective way for FL emission [10]. Wu's group [12] prepared long-lived room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials with a lifetime up to 0.3 s using boric acid as the precursor. The highly-efficient RTP is from delocalization of n electrons of oxygen atoms in packed boric acid solids through space conjugation. However, current studies of polymerization-induced emission (PIE) are mainly focused on the macromolecular polymer, while the “atypical” organic small molecules are rarely reported. Therefore, exploring the application of “atypical” organic small molecules in the construction of highly-fluorescent materials will provide new insights into the mechanism of intriguing FL enhancement for organic small molecules, and help us to develop effective and facile strategies for the synthesis of functional materials with superior FL.

Generally, the finer structure of fluorescent material is hard to be achieved due to its solid structure. On the contrary, porous materials with dense pores are of scientific and technological interest due to their unique porous morphology and their interaction with the surrounding atoms, molecules, ions, nanoparticles. This interaction occurs not only on their external surface but throughout the bulk of the material [16–18]. Therefore, the synthesis of porous materials with strong FL is of particular interest, with potential applications in drug release, optical display, catalysis, and so on [19]. General doping strategy, mixing exogenous luminescent nanomaterials or dyes into the synthesis of porous materials, may not only quench the FL of luminescent materials but also change the structure of porous materials [20–21]. Therefore, the direct and one-pot synthesis of luminescent porous materials is crucial to obtain high luminous performance with fine porous structure.

In this work, we constructed highly luminescent gadolinium micro-networks (Gd-MNs) via hydrothermal carbonization of non-luminescent Gd3+ ions / citric acid (Gd3+/CA) complexes which are based on the proposed hyperchrome-enhanced FL (HEF) effect. The HEF effect transforms the non-fluorescent small molecules into strongly fluorescent materials by converting the n-π* transition into strong π-π* transition, responsible for significant enhancement of light absorption ability. This new photoactivation strategy can effectively improve the FL of Gd-MNs with an enhancement of about 152-times compared to that of Gd3+/CA complexes. The porous Gd-MNs (P-Gd-MNs) with tunable porosity can be achieved by varying reaction temperature. The coupling of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) into the porous structure of P-Gd-MNs can be used for the preparation of white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) with adjustable correlation color temperatures (CCTs), from cold white light to warm white light. Meanwhile, Cu coated P-Gd-MNs (Cu@P-Gd-MNs) is a good peroxidase-like catalyst for the degradation of rhodamine 6G (R6G) dye.

Gadolinium(III) nitrate hexahydrate, R6G and triethylenetetramine (TETA) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). CA was purchased from Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was commercially available from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were dissolved using 18.2 MΩ.cm ultrapure water (EMD Millipore, Shanghai).

The absorption spectra of Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs were measured through a Lambda 950 UV–visible-near infrared spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The elemental mapping of Gd-MNs was measured using an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) used in combination with a field emission scan electron microscopy. The surface element compositions of Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs were measured with an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi). The FT-IR spectra of CA, Gd3+/CA complex, Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs were collected on a Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (Hitachi FTIR 8400S). The crystal phase of Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs could be measured through a rotating anode X-ray powder diffractometer (max2550VB). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs were obtained with a Tecnai G2 F20 field emission transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). The FL images of P-Gd-MNs were obtained by a laser confocal microscope (Leica, TCS SP8). Thermogravimetric curves of Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs were determined through a thermogravimetry (TGA 8000). The FL spectra of CA, Gd3+/CA complex, Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs could be measured through a FL spectrophotometer (LS-55 Lumine). The FL lifetimes of Gd3+/CA complex, Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs were measured with a FL-TCSPC FL spectrophotometer. The absolute FL quantum yields (QYs) of Gd3+/CA complex, Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs were measured through an absolute QY spectrometer (Quantaurus-QY, Hamamatsu).

The preparation of Gd-MNs was facile by a one-pot hydrothermal method. Firstly, 0.6 g CA and 0.4 g Gd(NO3)3⋅6H2O were added into a clean autoclave, and then dissolved in 5.0 mL water. The obtained solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflonlined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 5 h. The autoclave was cooled down naturally, and followed by purification. Due to the large size of the Gd-MNs, Gd-MNs could not be dispersed in aqueous solution and was easy to precipitate. After the reaction solution was removed, the resulting products were precipitated and suspended for five times with distilled water. Finally, the resulting products were dried by vacuum drying oven to obtain fluorescent Gd-MNs powder for further application. For the synthesis of P-Gd-MNs, only the reaction temperature need to be raised to 210–240 °C, and the other conditions were the same as the synthesis of the Gd-MNs.

The synthesis of CDs@P-Gd-MNs, including CDs-1@P-Gd-MNs, CDs-2@P-Gd-MNs, CDs-3@P-Gd-MNs could be achieved by the hydrothermal carbonization of R6G and TETA in the presence of P-Gd-MNs. In short, 0.3 g P-Gd-MNs powder was added into a clean autoclave, and then 1 mL TETA and 4 mL R6G (0.05 M) were added into the autoclave. The obtained mixture was transferred into a 25 mL Teflonlined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After five times of natural precipitation and suspension processes, the resulting products were dried by vacuum drying oven to obtain CDs-1@P-Gd-MNs powder. For obtaining CDs-2@P-Gd-MNs and CDs-3@P-Gd-MNs, the volume of TETA and the concentration of R6G were changed to 0.5 mL TETA/4 mL R6G (0.05 M) and 0.2 mL TETA/4 mL R6G (0.05 M), respectively. The other conditions were the same as the synthesis of the CDs-1@P-Gd-MNs.

The synthesis of Cu@P-Gd-MNs could be achieved by mixing the Cu2+ ions solution and P-Gd-MNs on a hydrothermal reaction. In short, 0.15 g P-Gd-MNs powder was added into a clean autoclave, and then 10 mL CuSO4⋅5H2O (0.05 M) was added into the autoclave. The obtained mixture was transferred into a 25 mL Teflonlined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After five times of natural precipitation and suspension processes, the resulting products were dried by vacuum drying oven to obtain Cu@P-Gd-MNs powder for further application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation process of R6G using Cu@P-Gd-MNs
ce:section.ce:para: The R6G could be degraded quickly in the presence of H2O2 using Cu@P-Gd-MNs. Firstly, we added 100 or 150 mg of Cu@P-Gd-MNs catalyst into a clean glass bottle. Then, 0.05 mL R6G dyes (0.05 M) and 1 mL H2O2 (30%) were added to the distilled water (0.95 mL) in turn. Finally, the FL intensity of R6G at different catalytic times was measured.

The 3D Gd-MNs are prepared by facile hydrothermal treatment from the mixture of Gd3+ ions and CA. The synthesis process is schematically displayed in Figure S1 , which shows the simplicity and high yield (19.9%) of this approach [22]. Time-dependent morphology evolution has been employed to investigate the growth process of Gd-MNs ( Fig. 1
a). The Gd3+ cation is well coordinated with the carboxyl and hydroxyl oxygen from the CA molecule ( Fig. 1 a, stage i). The change in FT-IR spectra confirms the coordination of Gd3+ cation with the CA molecule. CA molecule ( Fig. 1 b, brown line) shows the characteristic absorption bands of O–H and C = O stretching vibrations at 3432 and 1730 cm−1, respectively [23–24]. After the formation of the Gd3+/CA complex ( Fig. 1 b, purple line), two strong bands appeared at 1615 and 1383 cm−1 which correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of the carboxylate groups [25], respectively, indicating good coordination between the carboxyl group and Gd3+ cation. Moreover, only CDs with blue FL can be formed when hydrothermal carbonization of CA alone (
Figure S2
), further indicating the coordination between the CA and Gd3+ cation. Meanwhile, a broad absorption band located at about 3411 cm−1 is related to the –OH groups associated with physisorbed H2O bound by a weak hydrogen bond [26–27]. Moreover, the absorption band located at about 3211 cm−1 corresponds to the coordinated H2O molecules that are strongly bound to the Gd3+ cation by Gd-O bond [26–27]. Therefore, a large number of H2O molecules are also involved in the coordination process.
Subsequently, Gd3+/CA complexes can be polymerized into 2D micro sheets ( Fig. 1 a, stage ii). At high

temperature, the residual uncoordinated –COOH and –OH groups are further coordinated with adjacent Gd3+ cation. This forms an extended 2D sheet structure that causes aggregation of Gd3+/CA complexes. The difference between the symmetric and asymmetric stretching of carboxylate groups (Δν=(νs(COO – ) - νas(COO – ))) is closely related to the coordination of rare-earth ions. [28] The Δν of Gd3+/CA complex is 232 cm−1, which is decreased to 170 cm−1 after the formation of Gd-MNs ( Fig. 1 b, blue line). This decrease reveals that CA molecules are effectively bonded to the Gd3+ cation. Meanwhile, the peak at 1724 cm−1 corresponds to C = O stretching which disappeared after the formation of Gd-MNs ( Fig. 1 b, blue line). Once the Gd-MNs are formed then this stretching shows more transformation from carboxylic acid to carboxylate. Comparative analysis of the above results and discussion led us to conclude that the formation of 2D microsheets is directly attributed to high temperature-promoted disordered aggregation of Gd3+/CA complexes.

The SAED pattern ( Fig. 1 g) of Gd-MNs shows dispersion ring patterns which indicates a non-crystalline structure [29]. Meanwhile, analysis of the XRD spectrum ( Fig. 1 h) reveals that Gd-MNs has an amorphous structure with a broad diffraction peak at about 42.8°. The elemental mapping including surface and section mapping ( Fig. 1 i) shows that the Gd-MNs contain C, O, and Gd elements. On the other hand, the distribution of these three elements on the surface and inside of Gd-MNs is also very uniformed. This uniform distribution indicates that Gd-MNs are homogeneously formed. Finally, the XPS spectrum ( Fig. 1 j) confirms that Gd-MNs consist of C, O, and Gd elements.

The P-Gd-MNs are synthesized by controlling the reaction temperature as depicted in Fig. 2
a. The Gd-MNs were prepared at 200 °C and compactly connected “hot-nodes” with a smooth surface. As the temperature of the hydrothermal process increases up to 210 °C then nanopores are appeared both in the interior and at the surface of P-Gd-MNs. The size of these pores further increases to about 90 nm when the temperature is further increased to 240 °C ( Fig. 2 b). Time-dependent morphology evolution further reveals that the pore size of P-Gd-MNs increased with the reaction time (
Figure S4
). SEM image of P-Gd-MNs at 240 °C shows a uniformly porous structure ( Fig. 2 c). Analysis of the SAED pattern ( Fig. 2 d) shows that P-Gd-MNs have

Furthermore, we investigate the formation mechanism of P-Gd-MNs. FT-IR spectrum of P-Gd-MNs has obvious differences compared to that of Gd-MNs as can be seen from Fig. 2 f. The high temperature (240 °C) prepared P-Gd-MNs show a sharp peak at 3419 cm−1 instead of a broadband (3410 cm−1) as found in Gd-MNs. This indicates that the increase in temperature reduces the hydrogen bonding between trapped H2O molecules [30]. Moreover, thermogravimetric analysis of Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs reveals the change of H2O molecule insides two MNs (see Fig. 2 g). The Gd-MNs show a rapid and segmented weight loss below 430 °C, which is due to the loss of physisorbed and coordinated H2O. However, the P-Gd-MNs have a slow weight loss, and higher weight percentage than that of Gd-MNs at 430 °C, indicating that P-Gd-MNs have lower H2O content. Meanwhile, the amount of O also decreases when Gd-MNs are converted to P-Gd-MNs ( Fig. 2 h) that reveals the reduction of H2O molecule. Thus, the formation of porous structures can be associated with the release of H2O molecules in P-Gd-MNs. A water-induced growth mechanism of P-Gd-MNs is shown in Fig. 2 i. During the formation of Gd-MNs, many of their H2O molecules are bonded through hydrogen bonding and coordination. At high temperatures such as 240 °C, H2O molecules vapourize from the Gd-MNs and result in porous structural P-Gd-MNs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hyperchrome-enhanced fluorescence effect of Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs
ce:section.ce:para:
The FL of Gd-MNs is uniformly distributed and shows their strong multi-color imaging ability as shown in Fig. 3
a. The 3D FL image (
Figure S6
) shows that Gd-MNs are spatially well-defined fluorescent assemblies. The FL quantum yield (QY) is about 19.7% for Gd-MNs, which is significantly higher than that of the corresponding Gd3+/CA complex (0.13%) ( Fig. 3 b). Therefore, it is found that Gd-MNs is 152 times efficient compared to Gd3+/CA complex. After the formation of P-Gd-MNs, the QY decreases to 9.5%. The reason behind this may be their porous structure which destroys the intrinsic conjugate structure and consequently decrease the luminescent efficiency.

Next, the HEF effect is counter-checked from the observation of change in FL lifetime. After the formation of Gd-MNs, the lifetime decreases from 7.59 ns (Gd3+/CA complex) to 3.95 ns (Gd-MNs) ( Fig. 3 e). Considering the change of QY, both the radiative and non-radiative rate constants increases from 1.7 × 105 s−1 (Gd3+/CA complex) to 5.0 × 107 s−1 (Gd-MNs) and from 1.3 × 108 s−1 to 2.0 × 108 s−1, respectively (see Table S1). The above results and discussion led us to predict that the FL enhancement is due to the significant increase of radiative rate, not the non-radiative one. Therefore, FL enhancement has a direct relation with the hyperchromic effect, rather than the typical AIE mechanism [35–37]. In the case of P-Gd-MNs, the radiative rate constant decreases while the non-radiative one increases. The radiative rate constant of P-Gd-MNs decreased to 3.2 × 107 s−1 and the rate of non-radiative one continuously increases up to 3.0 × 108 s−1 (Table S1). This result confirms that the strong FL emission of Gd-MNs is because of HEF effect (Fig. 3 f). In addition, an n-π* transition is found in the CA molecule and Gd3+/CA complex under the excitation of UV light. Furthermore, this n-π* transition converts to π-π* in Gd-MNs, which significantly enhance their light absorption capacity and eventually the FL emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs@P-Gd-MNs for WLEDs with adjustable CCTs
ce:section.ce:para:
The facile and gram-scale synthesis of Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs are responsible for their high potentials in WLEDs. In order to evaluate the device performance of Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs, we fabricated Gd-WLEDs (Gd-MNs as the emitter) and P-WLEDs (P-Gd-MNs as the emitter) by combining UV chips (365 nm). Since the emission of Gd-MNs is mainly in the blue region (
Figure S10
), Gd-WLEDs has a very high correlation color temperature (CCT) of 15427 K under a drive current of 60 mA (Table S2). The P-WLEDs emit a typical cold white light with a CCT of 8431 K under a drive current of 60 mA as shown in Fig. 4
a. This is due to the red-shifted emission as shown in the Supporting Information of Figure S11 .

with a CCT of 2026 K (warm WLEDs) ( Fig. 4 a). The chromaticity coordinates of five typical WLEDs, (0.2405, 0.293), (0.2755, 0.345), (0.2932, 0.3979), (0.3578, 0.3529), and (0.4733, 0.3457) are marked in CIE 1931 colour spaces, respectively ( Fig. 4 b and Table S3). Photographs of four WLEDs (P-WLEDs, CDs-1@P-WLEDs, CDs-2@P-WLEDs, and CDs-3@P-WLEDs) are shown in Fig. 4 c.

A high-resolution SEM image of P-Gd-MNs confirms the existence of CDs in their porous structure. As shown in Fig. 4 d, the surface of P-Gd-MNs contains a large number of nano-sized CDs particles, as indicated by the blue arrows. Mapping of N elements from CDs ( Fig. 4 e) indicates that CDs are uniformly distributed in the porous structure. The CDs prepared using TETA and R6G show strong absorption in the UV region with a long tail extending into the visible region (
Figure S15
a). Moreover, 3D FL spectrum shows that CDs have a maximum emission at 538 nm when excited at 509 nm (
Figure S15
b). Generally speaking, the FL of CDs can be quenched to aggregate state due to non-radiative Forster energy transfer [5,38]. However, we found that the CDs of the P-Gd-MNs' surface can still emit strong FL, which maybe due to their isolated porous structure [39]. Although, the luminescent efficiency of the as-fabricated WLEDs should be further improved, the facile preparation, gram-scale synthesis, adjustable CCTs, and low-cost synthesis of P-Gd-MNs, have great potential applications in the field of WLEDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu@P-Gd-MNs as peroxidase-like catalyst for R6G dye degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
The presence of CA residues on surfaces of P-Gd-MNs can serve as a reducing reagent for the reduction of metal ions [6]. At high temperatures, Cu2+ ions can be reduced by P-Gd-MNs to form Cu coated P-Gd-MNs (Cu@P-Gd-MNs). Furthermore, Cu@P-Gd-MNs have good peroxidase-like catalytical activity, which can be used for the catalytical degradation of R6G dye ( Fig. 5
a). SEM image of Cu@P-Gd-MNs clearly reveals that their surface is evenly covered by Cu ( Fig. 5 b).

In this work, we propose HEF effect for the construction of highly fluorescent Gd-MNs with a significant FL enhancement of about 152-folds compared to its Gd3+/CA complex precursor. It is found that P-Gd-MNs have tunable porosity which can be achieved by varying the reaction temperature. A water-induced growth mechanism is proposed for the formation of P-Gd-MNs. Importantly, our synthetic design of CDs@P-Gd-MNs can be employed for the fabrication of a series of WLEDs from cold white light to warm white light. Finally, Cu@P-Gd-MNs show good peroxidase-like catalytical activity with a degradation efficiency of 100%. Therefore, P-Gd-MNs will have broad applications in WLEDs and can also be used for the treatment of dye-contaminated wastewater. Besides, the densely distributed pores of P-Gd-MNs provide a large storage room for functional molecules, which can be potentially applied for drug delivery in the future.

This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21977031, 21788102, 21777041, 21974046), the Shanghai Science and Technology Committee (17520750100, 19391901700, 19520744000 and 19ZR1472300) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Indicate that Supporting Information is included using the following statement: Supporting Information is available and includes [morphology and characterization of Gd-MNs and P-Gd-MNs, FL spectra, photoelectric parameters of WLEDs, catalytic efficiency]. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.129959.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
